On this page

Enums and Pattern Matching

Enums and Pattern Matching

Section titled “Enums and Pattern Matching”

Enums allow you to define a type by enumerating its possible variants.

Enum value

Section titled “Enum value”

We attach data to each variant of the enum directly

info

You can put any kind of data inside an enum variant:

  • strings
  • numeric
  • structs
  • enum

Defining Enum with different type of its variants is similar to defining different kind of struct. Different is that all variants are grouped under the Enum type

Also if we used the different structs, each of which has its own type, we couldn’t as easily define a function to take any of these kinds of messages as we could with the Message enum, which is a single type.

Defining Method

Section titled “Defining Method”

We can also define method on enums using impl

Option Enum

Section titled “Option Enum”

Variable that have type Option<T> means it either contain T or None (a value that can possibly be null)

In order to use a value T, you are required to explicitly handle the case when the value is null.

info

Option enum is an enum defined by the standard library.

Some and None variants are also include in the prelude.

You can use directly without the Option:: prefix.

match Expression

Section titled “match Expression”

match allows you to compare a value against a series of patterns and then execute code based on which pattern matches.

The code associated with each arm is an expression , and the resultant value of the expression in the matching arm is the value that gets returned for the entire match expression .

match end when a particular pattern is matched.

info

Every possible case of match need to be handled.

Tuple variant enum with match

Section titled “Tuple variant enum with match”

If your enum variant have a tuple as a value, you can use this syntax to access the variant’s value:

state value will be UsState::Alaska

Matching with Option<T>

Section titled “Matching with Option<T>”

Catch-all Pattern

Section titled “Catch-all Pattern”

Defining a variable as a pattern in match will match all values not specifically listed.

If the value will not be used, prepend variable name with _ or use _ as a variable name

if let

Section titled “if let”

if let is a syntax sugar of match but it can express only 1 pattern per if let

note

if let does not have the exhaustive checking that match enforces.


Sources

Section titled “Sources”
  • https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html
  • https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-02-match.html
  • https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-03-if-let.html
